chapter_title: Type Conversion, Type Aliases, and Type Deduction

cards.back: A type conversion between fundamental types that is NOT a numeric promotion. Unlike promotions, numeric conversions may or may not be safe and may result in data loss.

cards.front: What are the 5 basic types of numeric conversions?
cards.back:
• Converting an integral type to any other integral type (except integral promotions)
  • Converting a floating point type to any other floating point type (except floating-point promotions)
  • Converting a floating point type to any integral type
  • Converting an integral type to any floating point type
  • Converting an integral or floating point type to bool

cards.text: A {{c1::narrowing conversion}} is a numeric conversion that may result in data loss, such as converting a larger type to a smaller type or a floating point to an integral type.

cards.question: What problem occurs in this code?
cards.answer: Compile error: Brace initialization disallows narrowing conversions.
cards.explanation: Converting double 3.5 to int would lose the fractional part (0.5). Brace initialization prevents this narrowing conversion to avoid data loss.

cards.question: What value does i get and why?
cards.answer: i gets value 3. The fractional part (0.5) is dropped when converting double to int using copy initialization.
cards.explanation: Copy initialization (using =) allows narrowing conversions. When converting floating point to integral, the fractional component is dropped (not rounded).

cards.text: When converting a floating point value to an integral type, the {{c1::fractional component}} is {{c2::dropped}} (not rounded).

cards.concept: Data loss from converting large integers to floating point
cards.explanation: Converting a large integer to a floating point type can lose precision because floating point types store a limited number of significant digits. Values that require more precision than the type can hold will be rounded.
examples: float f = 123456789;  // float has ~7 significant digits

cards.question: What unexpected behavior occurs here?
cards.answer: Prints a large positive number (like 4294967295 on 32-bit systems).
cards.explanation: Converting a negative signed int to unsigned int causes the value to wrap around. -1 becomes the maximum value representable by unsigned int due to modulo wrapping.

cards.front: How do integral and floating point values convert to bool?
cards.back: Value 0 (or 0.0) converts to false. Any non-zero value converts to true.

cards.text: When converting a numeric value to bool, {{c1::0}} becomes false and {{c2::any non-zero value}} becomes true.

cards.front: Why should you avoid narrowing conversions when possible?
cards.back:
Narrowing conversions are unsafe because they can result in data loss:
  • Loss of fractional values when converting float to int
  • Out of range values when converting larger to smaller types
  • Sign changes when converting signed to unsigned
  • Loss of precision when converting large int to float

cards.question: What happens in this conversion?
cards.answer: Undefined behavior or implementation-defined behavior. The value 30000 is outside the range of char (-128 to 127), resulting in overflow.
cards.explanation: Converting a value that doesn't fit in the target type's range produces undefined or implementation-defined behavior, often resulting in a wrapped or truncated value.

cards.text: When converting floating point to integral type, the {{c1::fractional part is dropped}}, not rounded.
When converting numeric types to bool, {{c1::0}} becomes {{c2::false}} and {{c3::non-zero}} values become {{c4::true}}.
